coredata.dc:title: Fe3+ and intracellular pH determination based on orange fluorescence carbon dots co-doped with boron, nitrogen and sulfur
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Shengmei
Hu, Junhui
Li, Minglu
Gong, Xiaojuan
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: The fluorescent boron, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (BNSCDs) were prepared by simple hydrothermal reaction of 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid at 200 °C for 8 h. The fluorescence of the BNSCDs could be quenched by Fe3+ based on the electron transfer between Fe3+ and BNSCDs, so a label-free, good selectivity and high sensitivity method for Fe3+determination was established with linear range and LOD of 1.5–692 μmol/L and 87 nmol/L, respectively. And then the fluorescent probe was employed for detection of Fe3+ in tap water, coal gangue, fly ash and food samples successfully. Moreover, the as-prepared BNSCDs could serve as a novel pH fluorescent probe in the range of pH 1.60–7.00, which could be attributed to the proton transfer of carboxyl groups on the surface of BNSCDs. More importantly, the pH fluorescent probe possesses fast, real-time and low toxicity, applying for intracellular pH fluorescence imaging in HIC, HIEC, LO2 and SMMC7721 cells. In view of its simplicity, timely response and outstanding compatibility, the as-fabricated BNSCDs show the potential applications in water quality and solid waste monitoring, food detection, real-time measuring of intracellular pH change in vitro.

(B) The emission spectra of the BNSCDs (0.45 mg/mL) under different excitation wavelengths. Both the excitation and emission slits are 10 nm. head.ce:title: Fe3+ and intracellular pH determination based on orange fluorescence carbon dots co-doped with boron, nitrogen and sulfur

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent boron, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (BNSCDs) were prepared by simple hydrothermal reaction of 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid at 200 °C for 8 h. The fluorescence of the BNSCDs could be quenched by Fe3+ based on the electron transfer between Fe3+ and BNSCDs, so a label-free, good selectivity and high sensitivity method for Fe3+determination was established with linear range and LOD of 1.5–692 μmol/L and 87 nmol/L, respectively. And then the fluorescent probe was employed for detection of Fe3+ in tap water, coal gangue, fly ash and food samples successfully. Moreover, the as-prepared BNSCDs could serve as a novel pH fluorescent probe in the range of pH 1.60–7.00, which could be attributed to the proton transfer of carboxyl groups on the surface of BNSCDs. More importantly, the pH fluorescent probe possesses fast, real-time and low toxicity, applying for intracellular pH fluorescence imaging in HIC, HIEC, LO2 and SMMC7721 cells. In view of its simplicity, timely response and outstanding compatibility, the as-fabricated BNSCDs show the potential applications in water quality and solid waste monitoring, food detection, real-time measuring of intracellular pH change in vitro.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have aroused special attention since they were discovered by Xu etc. [1] in 2004, because they have superior optical properties [2], superior water solubility [3,4], low toxicity [3–5], outstanding biocompatibility [3–7] and wide application areas [3–15]. Many methods such as arc discharge [1], electrochemical [8], chemical oxidation [2,9], hydrothermal [10], microwave [16], etc. for the fabrication of CDs have been developed, thereinto, hydrothermal method was widely used to synthesis CDs because of its high repeatability.

Iron (Fe) is the important element in the earth's crust, which is rank fourth only inferior to oxygen, silicon and aluminum. Fe is also one of the essential microelements for people and animal, participating in many physiological functions, such as transporting and storing oxygen, accelerating growth, enhancing resistibility, preventing fatigue and treatment of iron deficiency anemia [17,18], so the balance of Fe in the human body is very important. Fe was taken in by means of diet to meet the needs of the growth and development of body [19], so it is necessary to fabricate a highly sensitive and selective probe for Fe determination in food, in order to provide the guiding significance in the choice of food. Many doped-CDs such as N-CDs [20–22], N,S-CDs [23,24] and N,S,B-CDs [25] have been used to determine Fe3+, attributing the doping of element in CDs could promote the luminescent properties of CDs [26,27]. And some agricultural products such as Jinhua bergamot [28], garlic [23], rose-heart radish [29], lycii fructus [30], bauhinia flower [31], sweet potato [32], mangosteen pulp [33] and black soya beans [19] were also used to prepare CDs by hydrothermal method, and these CDs could be used to detect Fe3+. Undoubtedly, these reported probes possess respective advantage, but we want to develop a novel Fe3+ probe with lower interference, better selectivity, higher sensitivity and faster response. And this probe could be used to detect Fe3+ in food samples and industrial waste with satisfying results.

pH is one of the most important physical and chemical parameters in aqueous solutions, which could affect the natural phenomena, chemical changes and production processes. Therefore, it is necessary to detect pH in industrial, agricultural, medical, environmental and biological fields [34]. There are many methods for detecting pH, such as nuclear magnetic resonance [35,36], spectrophotometric [37,38], colorimetric [39–41] and fluorescence method [42,43]. The fluorescence analysis method is concerned attributing to it possess excellent sensitivity, selectivity and simplicity. Furthermore, CDs are favored for their wonderful water solubility and biocompatibility as pH fluorescent probes. Currently, nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) [33–53], N,Eu-CDs [54], N,B-CDs [55], N,P-CDs [33,56], N,F-CDs [57] and N,S-CDs [58] have been used to construct the pH fluorescent probes. These probes put up a good performance in pH detection, while the short emission wavelength of CDs induces the interference in the determination of pH in the physiological environment. Therefore, it is an urgent need to provide a long-wavelength emission of CDs for pH detection in living cells.

In this paper, a brand-new and multi-targets responsive boron, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (BNSCDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (Scheme 1 ). The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the BNSCDs are 571 nm and 597 nm, respectively, and the fluorescence quantum yield is about 11.5%. The fluorescence of the BNSCDs could be efficiently quenched by Fe3+ in ultrapure water, so a method for detection of Fe3+ in the range of 1.5-698 μmol/L was established and used to Fe3+ determination in food samples and industrial waste with satisfying results. Moreover, the fluorescence intensities of the as-prepared BNSCDs have an immediate response to pH in the range of 1.60–7.00, serving as a pH probe to determine the change of the intracellular pH in vitro. The as-synthesized BNSCDs could open up more analytical applications in probing, bio-imaging, food quality monitoring and industrial waste recycling.

Nigericin and cresyl violet were purchased from Invitrogen (California, USA) and Acros Organics Bvba (Geel, Belgium), respectively. 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid, 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), 20 kinds of metal ions (KCl, NaCl, PbCl2, AgNO3, FeCl3, AlCl3, FeCl2, CaCl2, MnCl2, MgCl2, CrCl3, CdCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, BaCl2, ZnCl2, SnCl2, HgCl2 and TiCl3), 14 kinds of anions (NaI, Na2S, NaNO3, Na2C2O4, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaBr, NaF, NaCl, Na2S2O3, Na2SO3, NaSCN, NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4), 21 kinds of amino acids (Ala, Leu, Ile, Trp, Gly, Ser, Cys, Tyr, His, Lys, Glu, Asn, Gln, Val, Phe, Thr, Arg, Asp, Pro, Met and Hcy) and other chemical reagents were acquired from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All reagents are analytical grade and used directly without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ·cm) from a Milli-Q Plus System (Millipore, USA).

Typically, 66.4 mg of 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 75.2 mg of 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid were sonicated in 10 mL of ultrapure water to form a homogeneous solution and then it was transferred into a Teflon reactor, which was kept at 200 °C for 8 h. The light yellow supernatant was obtained by filtration and then it has been dialyzed for 12 h by dialysis membrane (1000 Da) and changed the ultrapure water every 3 h. Finally, the yellow powder was obtained after lyophilization.

All characterizations of the as-synthesized BNSCDs such as X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), etc., referring our previously published articles [6,20,51,52,58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Label-free determination of Fe3+ based on BNSCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of BNSCDs, 500 μL BNSCDs (5 mg/mL) was added into a 1-cm cuvette with 2 mL ultrapure water, the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs (1.0 mg/mL) solution was measured. Then twenty species of metal ions (20 μL, 0.1 mol/L), fourteen types of anions (20 μL, 0.1 mol/L) and twenty-one kinds of amino acids (100 μL, 0.01 mol/L) were respectively mingled with the BNSCDs solution and then the fluorescence intensities were recorded. To evaluate the interference of metal ions to Fe3+ determination, each 100 μL of the test ions solution (0.1 mol/L) was mixed with 2.4 mL BNSCDs (0.83 mg/mL) then the fluorescence intensities and spectra were estimated. Subsequently, 10 μL of Fe3+ (0.1 mol/L) was added into the above mixture solution, and then the fluorescence intensities and spectra were measured. In order to investigate the pH effect on Fe3+ determination, different pH of Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solution was prepared. 100 μL of 5 mg/mL BNSCDs in 2.00 mL of B-R buffer was mixed with 10 μL of 0.1 mol/L Fe3+, and then the fluorescence intensity was measured. To access the linear range and limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+, different concentrations of Fe3+ was mixed with 2.4 mL BNSCDs (0.83 mg/mL), and then the fluorescence intensity and spectra were estimated. All the samples were excited at 541 nm, and the fluorescence emissions were recorded at 597 nm unless otherwise stated.

For the determination of Fe3+ content in fly ash and coal gangue, 0.1 g of fly ash or coal gangue sample was placed in a Teflon stainless-steel autoclave. Then, 6.00 mL of nitric acid and 2.00 mL of hydrofluoric acid were orderly added into the reactor and treated for 10 min by ultrasound. Then the reactor was heated at 160 °C for 4 h in an oven to obtain a clear solution. Finally, the solution was diluted to an appropriate concentration in the detection range with ultrapure water.

To detect the content of Fe3+ in fresh fruits and vegetables, all samples were purchased from a vegetable market in Taiyuan. The pretreatment method of the samples (dry kelp, dry fungus, celery, mulberry and purple grape) was as follows. Firstly, the samples were washed with ultrapure water and weighted accurately after drying. Then the samples were crushed by a small crusher and the powder was dissolved in ultrapure water and centrifuged at 10 °C by a refrigerated centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 8.0 min. Finally, the stock solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm pore size filter membrane and then diluted to an appropriate concentration in the detection range with ultrapure water.

For the detection of pKa, a series of B-R buffer solution with pH from 1.60 to 13.00 were prepared. 2.00 mL of different pH of B-R buffer was mixed with the appropriate amount of BNSCD and shaken to mix uniformly in a 1-cm cuvette, and then the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured. The graph was obtained based on pH and the fluorescence intensity as abscissa and ordinate, respectively. The pKa of BNSCDs was calculated by Boltzmann fitting equation. In order to investigate the invertible cycle times, 6.0 M HCl and 1.0 M NaOH were used to regulate the pH of the BNSCDs between acidic and alkaline, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded accordingly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity assay and intracellular pH imaging in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: Human normal liver cell line LO2 (LO2) was used to research the cytotoxicity of the BNSCDs, and the experimental method was referred to our previous literatures [6,20,51,52,58].

Human small intestine cancer cell (HIC), human intestinal epithelial cell (HIEC), human liver cancer cell (SMMC7721) and LO2 were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), and then seeded in the confocal dishes at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere for 10 h to adhere on the confocal dishes.

For detecting the entry time of BNSCDs in cells, the confocal dish adhered with LO2 cells were washed three times by pH 7.40 PBS and kept in 1.0 mL PBS for laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) imaging. Subsequently, 100 μL of 5 mg/mL BNSCDs were added into the confocal dish to start dynamic scanning immediately by a Zeiss LSM880 LSCM.

For the investigation of the intracellular imaging under different pHs, the confocal dishes adhered with LO2 cells were washed three times by high K+ buffer (30 mM NaCl, 120 mM KCl, 1 mM CaCl2, 0.5 mM MgSO4, 1 mM NaH2PO4, 5 mM glucose, 20 mM HEPES and 20 mM NaOAc) at pH 3.00, 4.00, 5.00, 6.00 and 7.00, respectively. Subsequently, the LO2 cells were co-incubated with 100 μL of 5 mg/mL BNSCDs, 10 μLof 1.0 mg/mL nigericin and 1.00 mL high K+ buffer solution under the above pHs at 37 °C for 35 min, respectively. Successively, the LO2 cells were washed three times by pH 7.40 PBS, and then the LCSM images were obtained on a Zeiss LSM880 microscopy.

To investigate the response of the BNSCDs to intracellular pH, the pHs of LO2 cells were regulated by NH4Cl and H2O2. The LO2 cells were cleaned three times by pH 7.40 PBS and maintained in 1.0 mL pH 7.40 PBS, and then 100 μL of BNSCDs (5 mg/mL) was added and kept for 35 min at 37 °C. Subsequently, the cells were washed 3 times to remove extracellular BNSCDs with pH 7.40 PBS. The cells were maintained in 0.97 mL or 0.99 mL pH 7.40 PBS to search the optimal LSCM imaging location. Then, 30 μL of NH4Cl (1.0 mol/L) or 10 μL of H2O2 (0.1 mol/L) was respectively added into the above-mentioned confocal dishes, and the dynamic LSCM imaging was executed immediately.

Elemental analysis was employed to inspect the elemental content of the BNSCDs. As shown in Table S2A, the BNSCDs is composed of C, H, B, N, S and O with the percentage of 32.14, 4.96, 8.63, 11.66, 11.53 and 31.10 wt%, respectively, revealing that it is co-doped with boron, nitrogen and sulfur successfully. The empirical formula for a BNSCD is approximately C16H30B5N5S2O12, which was depicted in Table S2B.

XPS is used to study the chemical composition of the BNSCDs. The XPS survey scan of BNSCDs (Fig. 2A) exhibits six peaks at 529,399, 283, 230, 194 and 166 eV, which should be ascribed to O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s, B1s and S2p, respectively. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2A) is disassembled into four peaks at 532.92, 531.80, 531.21 and 530.92 eV, which are corresponding to O=C-O/SO4 2−, pyridinic-like O/S-O, C-OH/N-O/C=O and C-O-C, respectively [6,20,51,52,58]. The N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B) depicts three peaks at 401.47, 401.14 and 399.96 eV, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen (NH), pyrrole nitrogen (N-C/N=N) and C=N/C-N-N, respectively [6,20,51,52,58]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of the BNSCDs in Fig. S2B displays four peaks at 288.64, 285.95, 284.76 and 283.44 eV, which are associated with N-C=O/O-C=O/C ≡ N, C-N/C-S/C-O-C, C=C/C-C and CB, respectively [6,20,51,52,58,59]. Fig. 2C depicts the B1s spectrum which can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 192.10, 189.15 and 185.86 eV, representing B1s states in BN, BO and CB, respectively [59–61]. The high-solution S2p spectrum in Fig. 2D demonstrates four peaks at 169.61, 168.92, 168.30 and 167.79 eV, which are attributed to the S2p1/2 and S2p3/2, demonstrating that sulfur is present mainly in the form of a CS bond in thiophene sulfur and -C-SOx (x = 2, 3, 4, sulfate) groups, respectively [30,59,61]. Therefore, these results further demonstrate that B, N and S have been successfully doped into the BNSCDs.

FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2E) was used to characterize the surface groups of the as-fabricated BNSCDs. Three absorption peaks at 3734, 3430 and 3071 cm−1 are the frequency doubled peak of CO, OH or NH bonds and unsaturated CH in CC stretching vibration, respectively [62]. The three peaks at 2967, 2868 and 2921 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of methyl and the asymmetric stretching vibration of methylene, respectively [62,63]. The peak at 2599 cm−1 is classified to the stretching vibration of NH in amine salts or SH [60,62]. The weak peaks at 2127 and 2084 cm−1 might belong to the stretching vibration of -C≡N and –SCN, respectively [62,64]. The peak at 1688 cm−1 implies the stretching vibration of CO in an amide bond, and the peaks at 1530, 1260 and 638 cm−1 confirm that N exists as a secondary amide in the BNSCDs [62]. Three strong peaks at 1615, 1586 and 1518 cm−1 and a weak peak at 1453 cm−1 affirm the skeleton vibration of benzene ring [62]. In addition, the weak peaks at 1408, 1341 and 1311 cm−1 and a shoulder peak at 1191 cm−1 express the stretching vibration of CN, CO and CS, respectively [61,65,66]. In particular, the three strong peaks at 1136, 1084 and 1026 cm−1 represent the bending vibration of OH in B-O-H, the stretching vibration of CB and the deformation vibration of B-O-H, respectively [61]. Two peaks at 821 and 611 cm−1 respectively signify the para substitution of the benzene ring and SO4 2− [23,62]. These data of surface functional groups recognized by FTIR agree with the results of XPS.

The size and morphologies of prepared BNSCDs were observed by TEM and AFM. Fig. 2F indicates that the BNSCDs are well-dispersed and mainly of spherical morphology. And the size distribution of the BNSCDs are in the range of 1.4–2.4 nm with an average size of 1.9 ± 0.3 nm, which is assessed by calculating 100 counts of the particles. The high-resolution TEM in the inset of Fig. 2F clearly showed that BNSCDs exhibit an identical lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which is ascribed to 100 crystal plane of graphite [6,23] indicating the high degree of carbonization of BNSCDs. XRD measurements were performed on a Bruker AXS using a CuKa line (λ = 1.5406 Å, 30 kV, 10 mA) (Fig.S2D). And the peaks located at 24° and 41° in the XRD patterns of samples are identified as the (002) and (100) diffraction peaks of the prepared BNSCDs, respectively [67–69]. Fig. 2G and Fig. S2C depict the AFM images of the BNSCDs that possess a narrow height distribution with a mean height of about 0.5–3.3 nm.

For search the wide applications of the BNSCDs, the optical stability was investigated exhaustively. Fig. S3A describes that the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs in ultrapure water is almost unchanged after continuous xenon arc light irradiation for 2 h, suggesting that the fluorescence stability of the BNSCDs in ultrapure water is super. The interesting phenomenon is the pH-dependent fluorescence behavior. As shown in Fig. 3A and B, with the pH varies from 2.00 to 7.00, the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs increases significantly but remain fairly constant at pH 7.00–12.00, indicating its potential application in wide pH environment. Also, the photo-stability of the BNSCDs under different pH (3.00, 5.00 and 7.00) was further investigated. Fig. S3B, S3C and S3D demonstrate that the fluorescence intensities of the BNSCDs are stable at pH 3.00, 5.00 and 7.00 even under the xenon arc light irradiation for 2 h. The remarkable pH response and photo-stability of the BNSCDs makes the characteristic of their wide applications (vide infra), especially in extreme environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Label-free determination of Fe3+ based on the BNSCDs
ce:section.ce:para: An interesting finding is that the BNSCDs could serve as a label-free probe for Fe3+ determination. Firstly, the selectivity of this probe was evaluated. The fluorescence response of the BNSCDs to twenty kinds of metal ions, fourteen kinds of anions and twenty-one kinds of amino acids were explored and the results were shown in Fig. 4A, S4A and S4B, respectively. It can be seen that among all these tested substances, only Fe3+ could significantly quench the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs while the other ions and amino acids displayed low or negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs, illustrating that the BNSCDs show high selectivity to Fe3+ over the other metal ions, anions and amino acid. Furthermore, the response of the BNSCDs to Fe3+ in different pH (B-R buffer, 2.00–13.00) was studied because of the BNSCDs is sensitive to pH. As depicted in Fig. 4B and C, only in ultrapure water, Fe3+ has a significant quenching on the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs, while the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs is nearly unchanged (<10%) under other pHs, demonstrating that the BNSCDs could serve as a fluorescence probe for Fe3+ detection in ultrapure water. Apart from the selectivity of the Fe3+ probe, the anti-interference performance is another important parameter for the evaluation of this probe. To monitor the effect of other interfering ions with Fe3+ detection, various metal ions at 10 times enhanced concentration was introduced into the BNSCDs. Fig. S5A depicts that no pronounced changes were observed in the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs expect Fe2+, indicating that the BNSCDs have excellent anti-interference ability towards Fe3+ determination. It is worth noting that Fe2+ is not stable in the environment and easy to translate into Fe3+, so this probe could be used to detect Fe3+ in real samples. The response time was investigated and the result shown in Fig. S5B, indicating that the response of Fe3+ to the BNSCDs could be quickly finished in 20 s.

Fluorescence titration experiment was employed to investigate the relationship between Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs and the results were shown in Fig. 5A and B. Fig. 5A shows the changes of the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs after the addition of various concentration of Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs gradually decreased with the addition of Fe3+ from 0 to 2090 μmol/L, signifying that Fe3+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of the BNSCDs. In addition, the F0/F of the BNSCDs increased linearly with the concentration of Fe3+ from 1.5 μmol/L (0.084 mg/L) to 692 μmol/L (38.752 mg/L) with a linear equation of F0/F = 1.01 + 0.00223 c(Fe3+) and a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9778, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the BNSCDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The K
SV and limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ are calculated to be 2.23 × 103 L/mol and 87 nmol/L, respectively. Fe3+ as one of the essential trace elements in human body, the RNI (recommended nutrient intake) for adult men and women are 5–9 mg/day and 14–28 mg/day was regulated by the World Health Organization (WHO) [70,71], respectively, so the as-constructed probe could be used to Fe3+ determination in food samples. Furthermore, the proposed analytical method was contrasted to the previous CDs-based fluorescence method for the determination of Fe3+, and the results are summarized in Table S3. The proposed analytical method here possesses comparable linear range and relatively low LOD.

UV–Vis absorption titration experiments were executed to investigate the interaction mechanism between Fe3+ and BNSCDs. Fig. 5C clearly declares that the new substances are formed as Fe3+ added into the BNSCDs solution. Firstly, the absorption peak of Fe3+ at 293 nm is attributed to a d → d transition of the ligand field perturbation of hydrated iron ions, which disappeared in the absorption spectrum of the mixture of BNSCDs and Fe3+. The broad absorption peak at 450–620 nm of the BNSCDs associated with the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of the BNSCDs, which was disappeared but the new absorption peaks at 459 and 489 nm were appeared when Fe3+ was added into the BNSCDs, demonstrating that Fe3+ was reacted with the carboxyl group of BNSCDs to generate the new substance. That is, the charge transfer occurred between Fe3+ and BNSCDs and a new ligand was generated between them, so the fluorescence of BNSCDs was quenched by Fe3+. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime was used to confirm the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence lifetimes of the BNSCDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were investigated and the results are diagramed in Fig. 5D. The fluorescence decay curves are well fitted by a three-exponential function Eq. (1) and the relevant data are revealed in Table S4.

Here, B, τ and A are the corresponding amplitude, time constant of the radiative decay channel and a constant, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of the BNSCDs reduced by about 10% after the addition of 0.74 mmol/L Fe3+ (Table S4), indicating that fluorescence probing belongs to the static quenching [72], and the reduced lifetime indicates an ultrafast BNSCDs/Fe3+ electron-transfer process and leads to quenching (Fig. 5D and Table S4). More importantly, when the concentration of Fe3+ was more than 692 μmol/L, the F0/F curve become crooked (Fig. 5B), indicating a new fluorescence quenching mode may be occurred. According to the reported literatures [19,73], static quenching was the primary mechanism when the low concentration Fe3+ was mixed with the BNSCDs, but the dynamic quenching was activated when the high concentration Fe3+ was mixed with the BNSCDs.

The feasibility of the proposed fluorescence method for the detection of Fe3+ in real samples was investigated with standard water, tap water, coal gangue, fly ash, dry kelp, dry fungus, celery, mulberry and purple grape. All the error analysis was based on a t-distribution (bilateral inspection) with a confidence of 95% (t0.05, 4 = 2.78) [34], as shown by Table 1 . In Table 1, for each sample, the first and second line (light blue background) are the measurement result of Fe3+ content of sample and sample with the added Fe3+, respectively. The Fe3+ contents in standard water, tap water, coal gangue, fly ash, dry kelp, fungus, celery, mulberry and purple grape detected by the constructed probe are 0.0259, 0.0152, 2632, 2815, 150.23, 98.45, 8.54, 42.56 and 9.13 mg/100 g, respectively. It is noteworthy that the quantitative spike recoveries for the detection of Fe3+ in these real samples ranged from 93.4 to 104.2% with the relative standard deviations (RSD) between 0.42% and 6.9% (n = 5). So the provided method has favorable RSD and high precision and reproducibility. Therefore, the BNSCDs are promising in label-free monitoring of the amount of Fe3+ in environmental water, industrial waste and food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH-responsive based on the BNSCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Another interesting finding is that the as-fabricated BNSCDs could be used as a novel pH probe, which was discussed in exhaustive pH buffer solution between 1.60 and 13.00, and the results were shown by Fig. 5A, demonstrating that the fluorescence spectra of the BNSCDs were changed at a broad pH range of 1.60–7.00 under λex of 541 nm. As depicted in Fig. 5B, a well fitted sigmoidal curve using the Boltzmann equation was obtained, and the dissociation constant pKa of the as-prepared BNSCDs was calculated to be 4.44 ± 0.02, and the confidence of this value exceeds 99.9% [34]. The fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs increases as pH increases from 1.60 to 7.00, which was depicted in Fig. 5B. A good linear relationship was found between fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs and pH (1.60–7.00) based on the equation and R2 are F/F0 = 0.9718 pH - 0.7764 and 0.9766, respectively, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the BNSCDs at pH 1.60 and other pHs, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed fluorescent method was compared with the previously reported CDs-based fluorescence analytical methods for the determination of pH [32,26,45–52,54–58], and the results are listed in Table S5.

To clarify the mechanism of the proposed pH probe, the absorption spectra of the BNSCDs at different pH (2.00–13.00) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 6C and D, when pH increased from 2.00 to 13.00, the -COOH moieties on the surface of the BNSCDs are deprotonated to -COO− and then a negatively charged “protective shell” is formed on the surface of the BNSCDs; consequently enhancing the fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, the π → π⁎ transition at 285 nm red-shifted to 302 nm and the n → π⁎ transition at 350 nm was disappeared, and surface functional groups at 525 nm blue-shifted to 480 nm, demonstrating that the surface functional groups of the BNSCDs were changed when pH varied from acidic to basic. In order to prove the above luminescence mechanism, the zeta potentials of BNSCDs in ultrapure water and different pH B-R buffer solution were obtained and showed in Table S6, which clarified that the BNSCDs exists in the form of carboxylic acid and carboxylate in the acidic and alkaline system, respectively. And reaching equilibrium at about pH = 5. This result is consistent with pKa = 4.44. More importantly, Fig. S6 displays the color of BNSCDs under visible and UV light at different pHs (2.00–13.00). It reveals that the color of the BNSCDs changed from orange to yellow with the pH increased from 2.00 to 13.00, which is consistent with the emission wavelengths of the BNSCDs at 597 nm and 588 nm when pHs are at 2.00 and 7.00 (Fig. 6A), respectively. The proposed fluorescence mechanism was illustrated in Fig. 6E [74]. The reversibility is highly desirable for monitoring pH changes in living organs. The pH of the BNSCDs is switched back and forth between 3.0 and 7.4 by using HCl (6.0 mol/L) and NaOH (1.0 mol/L) solutions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6F, the fluorescence intensities of the BNSCDs are fully reversible and the response and recovery times in different pH solutions are rapid and within seconds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity assay and intracellular pH fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: For further using the BNSCDs in biological fields, the cytotoxicity of the as-fabricated BNSCDs to LO2 cells was discussed by the classical MTT assay. Fig. S7 reveals the viability of LO2 cells are higher than 80% even in the presence of a high concentration of the BNSCDs at 500 μg/mL for 24 h, suggesting the naturally low cytotoxicity of the BNSCDs, so that it could be used for imaging of live cells or other biomedical applications.

The kinetics of the BNSCDs in live cells was investigated by LSCM to expand the application of the as-prepared BNSCDs. As shown in Fig. S8, after LO2 cells were incubated with the BNSCDs for 35 min, LO2 cells emitted strong fluorescence and could not further strengthen when the incubation time prolonged, illustrating that the kinetic equilibrium time of the BNSCDs in LO2 cells is 35 min. So 35 min was the optimal incubation time for the BNSCDs and further used in the subsequent experiments.

In order to investigate the response speed of the BNSCDs to pH in live cells, the LSCM dynamics scanning experiments of LO2 cells incubated with the BNSCDs and then stimulated by NH4Cl and H2O2 were performed, respectively, and the results were depicted in Fig. 7A and B. Fig. 7A indicates that LO2 cells emitted strongest and weakest orange emissions after NH4Cl was added into the confocal dish for 1.0 min and 3.5 min, respectively, which may correspond to intracellular pH gradually increase and decrease, because NH4Cl could cause the change of intracellular pH [75]. Similarly, when H2O2was added into the confocal dish (Fig. 7B), the orange emissions emitted by LO2 cells gradually become weak and then keep unchanged after 3.0 min, demonstrating that intracellular pH was dropped by H2O2 stimulating, which is consistent with the reported results [75]. These results illustrate that the as-prepared BNSCDs could be employed as a novel intracellular pH fluorescent probe for live cell imaging and it can achieve an instant response to intracellular pH.

Furthermore, the LSCM images of LO2 cells incubated with the BNSCDs at high concentration of K+ buffer and nigericin at pH 3.00, 4.00, 5.00, 6.00 and 7.00 were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 7C, the cells stained with the BNSCDs display strongest and weakest orange emissions at pH 7.00 (the fifth panel of Fig. 7C) and 3.00 (the first panel of Fig. 7C), respectively, which is consistent with the results of the fluorescence pH probe (vide supra). For further exploring the biological application of the as-prepared BNSCDs, the fluorescence imaging comparison between normal cells and cancer cells was performed and the results were shown by Fig. 7D. HIC, HIEC, SMMC7721 and LO2 as model cells were employed for LSCM imaging, demonstrating that the as-prepared BNSCDs could enter into the normal and cancer cells.

Herein, fluorescent boron, nitrogen, and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (BNSCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal reaction with 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid for 8 h at 200 °C. A label-free, good selectivity and high sensitive fluorescence probe for Fe3+ determination was constructed based on the fluorescence of the BNSCDs could be quenched by Fe3+, which may be attributed to the electron transfer was occurred between the –COOH on the surface of the BNSCDs and Fe3+, and the linear range and LOD are1.5–692 μmol/L and 87 nmol/L, respectively. Furthermore, the Fe3+ in tap water, coal gangue, fly ash and food samples were determined successfully. More importantly, the fluorescent BNSCDs have a good linear relationship with pH in the range of pH 1.60 to 7.00 based on proton transfer, which could be applied in intracellular pH determination, possessing low toxicity, fast and real-time response. In a word, the probing concept introduced in this work is expected to be applied in the determination of Fe3+ in environmental water samples, industrial wastes and food samples, and pH detection in various biomedical domains, attributing to its simplicity, selectivity, sensitivity and immediate responsiveness.
